items.title: How to distinguish prerenal AKI from acute tubular necrosis
items.content:
Prerenal AKI reflects hypoperfusion with structurally intact tubules; ATN reflects intrinsic tubular injury. The fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) and urine microscopy help separate them.

Prerenal AKI: FENa <1%, bland sediment, urine osmolality >500 mOsm/kg, rapid response to volume repletion.

ATN: FENa >2%, muddy brown granular casts, sluggish recovery over days to weeks.

Diuretics and contrast can blur FENa — use FE urea when diuretics have been given.
keyPoints: FENa <1% favors prerenal; >2% favors ATN (if not on diuretics).
Muddy brown casts are classic for ATN.
items.reference: Acute Tubular Necrosis: "The fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) is typically >2% in ATN and <1% in prerenal azotemia, though diuretics and contrast may confound interpretation."
